speaker: interviewer
text: 1995, Interview with Olga Kovacs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Is there anything else you would like to tell us about your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, about-- about mother. That I'm sure she would have been terribly upset and sad to know that her only child will bury her. But I felt, I-- I just-- I just couldn't let anybody do it for her. And that she liked carnations, and there were a bunch of carnations. And then I sent them off, too. And I asked a fisherman to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] break up the urn with the wooden box into small pieces, and cut up the velvet covering cloth [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was put on the box. Tape it together, put some heavy stone or brick, or something around it, so that when we drop it, it should sink to the bottom of the sea. I didn't want anybody to use either the urn or the wooden container. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] For a while, I was thinking about keeping her ashes at home forever. But then, this would be against everything she believed in. She would have said, [INAUDIBLE], this is idolatry. You mustn't do it. So I gave this up. And also, it was her wish to have her ashes scattered. I got a piece of paper from the-- an official piece of paper from the fisherman, in whose boat we went out to sea, identifying the exact coordinates where we buried mother. And both my aunts and I put it in our will that, when time comes, we want to go-- we want to-- them to go through this for us, the lawyers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To bury us and scatter our ashes, and break up the container, and cut up the velvet, and sink it to the bottom of the sea, exactly under the same coordinates-- although I know that the currents take over immediately upon impact. But this way, the three of us-- all Auschwitz survivors, mother, her sister, and I-- will ultimately share the same fate. [CRYING] As an epilogue, I only would like to say that [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I'm only a survivor, but my family and the six million are the victims. And their lives and martyrdom [SOBBING] must never be forgotten. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] They're always with me. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] And I must be very grateful, in spite of all this tragedy, that at least I stayed with Mother and Auntie. And also that, after Auschwitz, for 48 more years, Mother was with us. It doesn't happen too often. It just happened a few times, at least among the people we have known, that mother and child, and the sister of the mother, were able to survive together. And I have to keep reminding myself, because sometimes the pain is unbearable. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Olga, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: For telling us your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think as time will go by, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it will ease the pain. It's like a memorial for them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Thank you, for everybody. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is the only picture of my father I have. His name was Dezso Kovacs. D-E-Z-S-O K-O-V-A-C-S. When we came back from Auschwitz, then the photograph in our hometown, Salgotarjan, told Mother that he still has the negative of the picture he did for Father. So Mother asked him for it. He gave us this picture, and then he blew up the negative so I have a large picture [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from Father. That's the only one we have.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This must have been taken, probably, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very late '30s and/or early '40s. I really don't remember. In Salgotarjan. The next picture is me. That was taken in Budapest, and I-- I really don't know how old I was here. Probably four, five, something like this. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Salgotarjan, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and it's a very happy occasion. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I believe sometime in 1933, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and it's the engagement of my aunt, Olga, to Dr. Laszlo Schwabach. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The first person on this picture--

speaker: interviewer
text: From the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Closing her eyes is my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please, [INAUDIBLE] from the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the left. IS my mother, sort of squinting and closing her eyes because of the flash. Next to her sit--

speaker: interviewer
text: The full name of your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mrs. Dezso Kovacs, born Sari Biheller. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Next to her is my aunt's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] future husband, Doctor Laszlo Schwabach, S-C-H-W-A-B-A-C-H. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Next to him, my aunt, Olga, also closing her eyes because of the flash, along with Laszlo. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Next to my aunt is my grandmother, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mrs. Jakob Biheller, born Cecilia Neu, N-E-U. In the middle, between Grandma and my grandfather, is my uncle, Mother and Auntie's brother, Jeno. Next to Jeno is my grandfather-- maternal grandfather-- Jakab Biheller. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then comes Aunt Irene, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mrs. Bela Biheller, born Irene Sonnenshein. Next to Irene is my uncle, Bela Biheller, and next to Bela is the witness of my future uncle, and the witness's name is Isthvan Roth, R-O-T-H. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] My father couldn't be in this picture. He had to go out of town and didn't make it in time back to town. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the photograph of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was taken during the wedding of Uncle Jeno with Lili.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Hungary, but I'm not sure about the city. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know whether it was in Salgotarjan. I really don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was taken, probably, in the late '30s. And in the background is my mother talking to Aunt Johanna, the sister of my grandmother, and Aunt Johanna's husband, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Uncle Isador Hollander, H-O-L-L-A-N-D-E-R. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is a picture of my cousin, Agnes, daughter of Jeno and Lili Biheller. That was taken probably in the early '40s, or in '39. I'm really not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was taken in Budapest. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The picture is of my uncle, Bela Biheller, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and his wife Irene Biheller, and their daughter Edith. I believe all these pictures were taken in Salgotarjan. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I cannot recall the date of Uncle Biheller's picture. Probably, again, the '30s. And Aunt Irene and Edith, again, late '30s or early '40s. [PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] This is me, one year after liberation, 1946. And this was supposed to be my immigration passport picture, when we did not succeed to come to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Salgotarjan.

speaker: interviewer
text: The middle one.

speaker: interviewee
text: The middle picture is of my aunt, Olga Schwabach, one year after Auschwitz. After liberation, more precisely. And it's supposed to be her passport picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother-- I just realized, this is a picture of Mother that was taken in Vienna in 1961 or 62. And this was the passport picture when we were still stateless. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The picture on the left is of my aunt, Olga, taken, I believe, in 1962. The gentleman in the background is Oscar Sunshine, the far relative who sent us the papers, twice, in order to help us to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Spring Valley. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next picture is-- on the left is my mother, on the right side is Aunt Olga. It was taken during the World's Fair in Flushing in 1964. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] These picture-- pictures are of my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I simply adored her. Mother was the greatest joy of my life. The picture on the left is probably from the '70s, and it's taken by me in a Regal Park, not far from where we lived. The next picture is, of course, in this apartment, and is from the 1980s. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's how I would like to remember her, smiling and arranging the flowers.
